plainMeaning: This verse simply names the seven sisters of the great monk Sthūlibhadra — Yakṣā, Yakṣadinnā, Bhūtā, Bhūtadinnā, Seṇā, Veṇā and Reṇā — remembering them as exemplary holy women.
recitation: jakkhā ya jakkhadinnā, bhūā taha ceva bhūadinnā ya | seṇā veṇā reṇā, bhayaṇīo thūlibhaddassa ||

wordByWord.gloss: Yakṣā (the first sister; a personal name)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jakkhā < Skt yakṣā, feminine of yakṣa ('luminous spirit'), here used purely as a proper name.
wordByWord.notes: Tradition: the eldest, who could retain a text after hearing it once.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ya < Skt ca, the connective conjunction.
wordByWord.notes: Links the first two names of the pair.

wordByWord.gloss: Yakṣadinnā (the second sister; a personal name)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jakkha-dinnā < Skt yakṣa-dattā, 'given by the Yakṣa'; dinnā < Skt datta ('given').
wordByWord.notes: Variant Yakṣadattā. Tradition: retained after hearing twice.

wordByWord.gloss: Bhūtā (the third sister; a personal name)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhūā < Skt bhūtā, feminine of bhūta ('being, spirit'); proper name.
Prakrit taha < Skt tathā ('thus, in that manner').
Prakrit ceva < Skt caiva (ca + eva), an emphatic conjunction.
wordByWord.notes: taha ceva together = 'and in the very same way'.

wordByWord.gloss: Bhūtadinnā (the fourth sister; a personal name)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhūa-dinnā < Skt bhūta-dattā, 'given by the bhūta (spirit)'.
wordByWord.notes: Variant Bhūtadattā. Tradition: retained after hearing four times.

wordByWord.gloss: Seṇā (the fifth sister; a personal name)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit seṇā; a proper name, sometimes connected with Skt senā ('host'). Some recensions read Eṇā.
wordByWord.notes: Tradition: retained after hearing five times.

wordByWord.gloss: Veṇā (the sixth sister; a personal name)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit veṇā; proper name (cf. Skt veṇā, a river-name / 'flute').
wordByWord.notes: Tradition: retained after hearing six times.

wordByWord.gloss: Reṇā (the seventh sister; a personal name)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit reṇā; proper name (cf. Skt reṇu, 'fine dust').
wordByWord.notes: Tradition: the youngest, who retained after hearing seven times.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhayaṇī (nom. pl. bhayaṇīo) < Skt bhaginī ('sister').
wordByWord.notes: The grammatical head that gathers the seven names into apposition.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit thūli-bhadda + genitive -ssa < Skt sthūlibhadrasya, 'of Sthūlabhadra' (sthūla 'great/stout' + bhadra 'blessed').
wordByWord.notes: The same Sthūlibhadra honoured among the great monks earlier in the litany.

literalTranslation: Yakṣā and Yakṣadinnā, Bhūtā and just so Bhūtadinnā, Seṇā, Veṇā, Reṇā — (these are) the sisters of Sthūlibhadra.
idiomaticTranslation: Yakṣā and Yakṣadinnā, Bhūtā and likewise Bhūtadinnā, then Seṇā, Veṇā and Reṇā — the seven sisters of Sthūlibhadra, holy nuns ever to be remembered.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the chief queens of Kṛṣṇa close the preceding gāthā, the litany turns from royal consorts to a single renunciant household, naming the seven sisters of Sthūlibhadra in one unbroken breath. The artistry is in the pairing: Yakṣā/Yakṣadinnā and Bhūtā/Bhūtadinnā fall as matched couplets (a 'name' and its '-dinnā / given' echo), and the half-line is then capped by the rapid triad Seṇā-Veṇā-Reṇā, so that seven names ride a single light metre. Only the last word, thūlibhaddassa, anchors them — they are remembered not for thrones or marriages but as bhayaṇīo, the sisters of a great monk. The verse is deliberately answered across the two panels of the sutra: Sthūlibhadra himself stands among the mahāsattvas in the men's roll, and here his seven sisters stand among the mahāsatīs — one family supplying exemplars to both halves of the recitation. Tradition remembers these seven as nuns of prodigious śruta-retention, graded by memory — the eldest holding a passage after a single hearing and each younger sister needing one hearing more, down to Reṇā at seven. The verse itself voices none of this; it trusts the bare names, true to the sutra's method of nāma-grahaṇa, to summon the whole remembered life. To 'take' these names is thus to honour an entire lineage of women who carried the canon in living memory.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The gāthā quietly asserts that great śīla and śruta are not the preserve of queens or wives but of ordained women — sādhvīs of the four-fold saṅgha. By placing seven nun-sisters beside the famous satīs, the verse affirms women as bearers and guardians of scripture in an age of purely oral transmission, and models renunciation that runs through a whole family (brother and seven sisters alike entering the path). It is remembrance, not petition: the reciter binds the day's resolve to women whose discipline preserved the teaching.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter pauses on these seven plain names and resolves that learning and steadfast conduct are open to all, women and men, lay and ordained; remembering nuns who held the canon by heart, one renews one's own commitment to svādhyāya (study) and to guarding what one has learned.

sources.title: Hemacandra, The Lives of the Jain Elders (Pariśiṣṭaparvan), trans. R. Fynes
sources.consultedFor: Narrative of Sthūlibhadra and his seven sisters as renunciants in the kathā tradition.

notes: Names are rendered verbatim from the book's Gujarati; common recension variants exist: Yakṣadinnā ≈ Yakṣadattā, Bhūtadinnā ≈ Bhūtadattā, and Seṇā is sometimes read Eṇā. The graded-memory tradition (eldest retains after one hearing, each younger sister one more, down to Reṇā at seven) is well attested in the Āvaśyaka/Pariśiṣṭaparvan cycle but is NOT stated in the verse itself, which gives only the names; it is offered as background, not as the gāthā's wording. The pairing of '-dinnā' names and the bare triad reflect the metre. No web source could be confirmed for an independent Anchalgachchha gloss of this specific gāthā; identifications rest on the standard Śvetāmbara kathā literature.
